content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 14, 1990


Peter A. Bagatelos
Bagatelos & Fadem
The International Building
601 California Street, Suite 1801
San Francisco, CA 94108

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-457

Dear Mr. Bagatelos:

This is in response to your letter of July 2, 1990, requesting advice regarding contribution limits under the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Specifically, you have requested information of certain telephone advice given by me in a conversation with you on July 2, 1990. Since you have not provided specific facts required for formal advice, this response to your letter will be in the nature of informal assistance, pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).²

QUESTIONS

1. What contribution limits apply when two general partners, each of whom has a 50 percent interest in a partnership (the "larger partnership") which controls 10-15 other partnerships (the "smaller partnerships"), each make an individual contribution to one candidate, and none of the partnerships contributes?

2. What contribution limits apply when one or more of the partnerships contribute to a single candidate?

3. What contribution limits apply when one or both of the individual partners and one or more of the partnerships make contributions to one candidate?


¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. I-90-457
Page 2

CONCLUSIONS

1. If the partners make joint decisions as to contributions
by the partnership, then neither partner has directed or
controlled the decisions to contribute and each partner
individually may give up to $1,000 to a single candidate in a fiscal year.

2. Business entities with the same controlling owner are
considered one person for purposes of cumulating contributions.
Hence the aggregated contribution from all the partnerships
controlled by the two partners herein cannot exceed a total of
$1,000.

3. If neither partner controls the decisions of any of the
partnerships to contribute, then each partner may contribute up to
$1,000. The partnership contributions may not aggregate more than
$1,000, whether or not either or both of the partners makes an
individual contribution.

FACTS

You represent a partnership owned by two 50 percent partners.
This partnership, in turn, controls 10-15 other partnerships.

Partnership decisions regarding political contributions are
made jointly by the partners. Neither partner controls partnership decisions or the other partner.

On July 2, 1990, you received telephone advice from me
regarding cumulation of contributions. You have written the Commission to obtain confirmation of the telephone advice in light of
your reading of the Olson Advice Letter, No. A-90-302 (copy
enclosed), which you perceive to be inconsistent with the advice
tendered to you on July 2, 1990.

DISCUSSION

The Act, as amended by Proposition 73, provides that
contributions from persons to candidates for elective office must
comply with fiscal year contribution limits set forth in Sections 85301, 85302 and 85303. The purpose of the contribution
limitation provisions of Proposition 73 was to place a reasonable
ceiling on the amount that could be contributed by one donor to a
candidate. See Recht Advice Letter, No. I-89-571 (copy enclosed)
citing Argument in Favor of Proposition 73, California Ballot
Pamphlet, June 7, 1988, Primary Election, p.34.

Under the Act there are two lines of authority which require
that contributions by more than one person be cumulated and
treated as single contributors. The standards for circumstances
requiring such cumulation were first set forth in 1976 in two

File No. I-90-457
Page 3

opinions, In Re Kahn, 2 FPPC Ops. 151 and In Re Lumsdon, 2 FPPC
Ops. 140 (copies enclosed). The Kahn Opinion concludes that a
cumulation of contributions is required (1) where contributions
were made by both a parent corporation and its wholly owned
subsidiaries, and (2) where the parent corporation made no
contribution, but the subsidiaries did contribute. Cumulation was
not required where surrounding circumstances made it clear that
the corporations made their contributions completely independently
of each other.

In the Lumsden Opinion, the Commission found that contributions of a closely held corporation and the majority shareholder
thereof required cumulation. In both Kahn and Lumsden, the
pivotal question was one of control of the contributions of one
entity by another entity or person.

More recent advice letters have been consistent with Kahn and
Lumsden. The Recht Advice Letter, supra, concerns, in part, the
cumulation of contributions by a partnership whose contributions
are controlled by a steering committee made up of five of 50
partners. Among the questions asked in the Recht letter was
whether the contributions of the partnership must be cumulated
with those of the five controlling partners. The advice given was
that since no single member of the steering committee actually
directed and controlled the partnership, cumulation of contributions made by the partnership and the individual members of the
steering committee was not required.

Further advice in the Recht letter involved a general
partnership consisting of two corporate partners. The contributions of the partnership were controlled by two individuals, one
of whom was president of one of the corporate partners and the
other, the chairman and chief executive officer of the same
corporation. In that factual situation, it was determined that
contributions of the partnership would not be cumulated with those
of the controlling individuals unless one of the individuals, in
fact, directed and controlled the contributions of the partnership
or the corporation.

In May, 1990, the Commission rendered advice in the Olson
Advice Letter, supra, to which you have made reference in your
letter. The Olson letter, as you correctly noted, concluded that
it was permissible for an official to accept contributions from
two general partners of a business entity even though the entity
itself had previously made a contribution of $1,000 to the
official's campaign. The reason for the decision rendered in
Olson was that neither of the two general partners of the business
entity who wished to make individual contributions that directed or
controlled the decision to contribute funds from the partnership
to the official; the third general partner who did direct and
control the entity's contribution would be required to cumulate
his or her contributions with that of the entity.

File No. I-90-457
Page 4

Regulation 18531.53 seeks to clarify the cumulation issue
where there is a question regarding cumulations of contributions
from affiliated entities. Regulation 18531.5 sets forth the following:

(a) If the same person or a majority of the
same persons in fact directs and controls the deci-
sions of two or more entities to make contributions
or expenditures to support or oppose a candidate or
candidates for elective office, those affiliated
entities shall be considered one person, one
political committee, or one broad based political
committee for purposes of the contribution limita-
tions in Government Code Sections 85301, 85302,
85303 and 85305.

(b) Business entities in a parent-subsidiary
relationship and business entities with the same
controlling (more than 50-percent) owner shall be
considered one person for purposes of the contribution
limitations in Government Code Sections 85301,
85302, 85303 and 85305, unless the business enti-
ties act completely independently in their deci-
sions to make contributions and expenditures to
support or oppose candidates for elective office.
For purposes of this section, a parent-subsidiary
relationship exists when one business entity owns
more than 50 percent of another business entity.

* * *

My advice to you in our conversation of July 2, 1990, was
consistent with both previous advice given in FPPC advice letters
and also with Regulation 18531.5. The first question you posited
concerned two equal partners who jointly decided that their
partnership entity should contribute $1,000 to a campaign. Since
neither partner directed or controlled the decision to contribute,
the entity's contribution would not be cumulated with those of the
individual partners, per Kahn, supra, Lunden, supra, Recht,
supra, and Olson, supra. Thus each partner would be permitted to
contribute $1,000.

The second query in your letter concerned cumulation of
contributions where one or more of the partnerships controlled by
your clients made contributions to the same candidate. Regulation

_______________________
3 Regulation 18531.5 is in the rulemaking process. Although not
currently effective, the regulation expresses the Commission's
policy concerning cumulation of contributions.

File No. I-90-457
Page 5

18531.5 requires cumulation of contributions when business entities are in a parent-subsidiary relationship or when business entities have the same controlling owner. Since the smaller partnerships are under the control of the larger partnership, the partnerships would be considered to be one person under Regulation 18531.5(b), and the maximum contribution from the combined partnerships would be $1,000. The amounts given by the different partnerships would not matter, so long as the sum of all the partnership contributions to one candidate for one fiscal year did not aggregate to more than $1,000.

Your final question postulates one or both of the individual partners and one or more of the partnerships making contributions to a single candidate. Again, if the decision regarding the contribution of any of the partnerships is made jointly, then either partner has controlled the decision and each partner may individually contribute $1,000. If the joint decision were to make a maximum contribution from any of the partnerships then that maximum could be made by any of the partnerships, but no further contribution from any of the partnerships would be permitted to the recipient candidate during that fiscal year.

If one of the partners does control the decision to contribute partnership funds, then any individual contribution by that partner would have to be aggregated with the funds contributed by the partnership. The other partner could make an individual contribution of up to $1,000 without any cumulation of partnership contributions.

The pivotal issue, in both the advice letters cited and also in Regulation 18531.5, is one of control. Where a business entity acts completely independently of any parent or affiliated entity in its decision to make a contribution to a candidate, there is no requirement to cumulate contributions.

The Olson letter, which you believe is inconsistent with the advice tendered to you in our telephone conversation, is actually quite consistent with what you were told, but Olson deals only with a matter analogous to the substance of your first question and does not touch on cumulation of contributions between entities. The conclusion in Olson is that two general partners who did not direct and control the contribution of a partnership did not have to aggregate their contributions with those of the partnership. This is precisely the advice given to you in our conversation of July 2, 1990.

File No. I-90-457
Page 6

     If you require further information from us regarding cumula-
tion of contributions, please do not hesitate to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]

                                        By:  Susan L. Bobrow
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:plh

Enclosures
